One house near the beginning of the track suffered structural damage losing a garage and utility room. Several homes also sustained roof damage. A few vehicles also received damage from falling trees or other debris. One person was injured when a tree fell onto a vehicle. A small steel outbuilding was also destroyed with debris from the outbuilding being wrapped in amongst surrounding trees. A support on a built in carport was destroyed allowing a portion of the home roof to collapse. Several power lines were also knocked down along Hamilton and Grubbs Springs Road. In total, 9 homes sustained major damage from the tornado with 35 homes receiving minor damage. In addition, 3 mobile homes sustained major damage and 7 received minor damage. One business was destroyed, one business sustained major damage and 3 received minor damage. Several vehicles were damaged as well. Timber damage alone from the tornado was reported to be around 92 thousand dollars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding across Marshall County. Ten roads sustained major damage. Seventeen roads received minor damage. Twenty-one homes and mobile homes sustained minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Horn Lake and Nesbit. Vehicles were stranded in flood water up to headlights at the intersection of Highway 302 and Highway 51. Motorists had to be rescued. The roads were closed as a result. In addition, a teenager had to be rescued from a flooded ditch off of Tulane Road near Nesbit. At the peak of the storm, one home in off of Dean Road had nearly three feet of water inside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle upper level disturbance combined with a very unstable air mass to produce numerous showers and thunderstorms across the Mid South on May 24th, 2010. As convection developed throughout the day outflow boundaries were created that resulted in additional development until nearly all of the Mid South experienced at least some precipitation by sunset. After sunset the storms dissipated. Some of the storms were severe with large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down power lines on the north side of Tunica. The winds also stripped away part of the North Tunica Fire Department's facade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located across West Tennessee and Arkansas during the afternoon and evening hours of May 16, 2010 while an upper level disturbance moved into the Mid Mississippi Valley. These features combined with daytime heating to produce showers and thunderstorms across the Mid South. Some of the storms were severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain produced flash flooding in Oxford. Many roads were covered with 3 to 4 feet of water throughout Oxford. Flooding damaged 21 homes, 7 apartments, 10 businesses and 25 cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heat wave occurred during the middle to latter part of June across the Mid-South as a strong upper ridge of high pressure moved over the region. Temperatures rose into the mid to upper 90s during the afternoon hours. The combination of highs in the mid to upper 90s and high humidity produced heat indices between 100 and 110 degrees. Overnight lows in the 70s to around 80 degrees provided little relief.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings ranged from 100 to 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours on June 26th, 2010 as a result of a very moist and unstable air mass combined with maximum daytime heating. The showers and thunderstorms would quickly dissipate after sunset with the loss of daytime heating. Some storms were severe producing damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked a tree on to a house on Woodland Heights on the west side of Aberdeen. Trees and power lines were knocked down on Matumba Street in the center of Aberdeen. A tree fell across Old Highway 25 between Aberdeen and Amory.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One small tree was blown down and it took down a power line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down in the Le Tourneau area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving weather pattern developed during the first two days of May and supported a couple rounds of thunderstorms both on Saturday and Sunday. While our area was spared the larger severe weather outbreak, we did manage to have a few rounds of severe storms on Saturday evening and then again Sunday afternoon. The most significant storms occurred Sunday afternoon and brought large hail, between golf ball and baseball sized, to portions of central Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding across a good portion of Webster County with 5 roads sustaining major damage with 2 of those washed out. A few dozen other roads had minor damage from flooding as well. One person was injured after driving through a flooded road and sinking into a hole from a washed out culvert. One home also sustained minor damage from flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm system moved across the region on April 24th and brought an outbreak of severe storms and tornadoes to the area. The most powerful and destructive storm during this event was the long track EF4 tornado which ripped a 149 mile path across the forecast area. In addition to this violent tornado, many other storms became severe and produced 5 additional tornadoes along with numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail. Of the 5 tornadoes, 3 of these were strong (EF2) and occurred across, Jasper, Newton, Lauderdale, Oktibbeha, and Clay Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down along Brown Road and tracked across Townsend Byrd Road before moving into Kemper County. Several homes were damaged along Frasier Grove Road as the tornado tracked to the northeast. The tornado also damaged or destroyed 4 barns, a shed, and uprooted numerous medium to large trees. Maximum winds were around 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle upper level disturbance combined with a very unstable air mass to produce numerous showers and thunderstorms across the Mid South on May 24th, 2010. As convection developed throughout the day outflow boundaries were created that resulted in additional development until nearly all of the Mid South experienced at least some precipitation by sunset. After sunset the storms dissipated. Some of the storms were severe with large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down power lines just south of Jonestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface heating combined with old outflow boundaries to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms southern parts of the Mid South during the late afternoon and early evening hours on May 25th, 2010. The storms dissipated a couple hours after sunset. A storm over Coahoma County produced isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Clarksdale. Several roads were flooded on the  west side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very dry conditions continued across west-central portions of Mississippi during July. The extended dry stretch resulted in an area of severe (D2) drought developing across parts of the Mississippi Delta. Embedded in that area, a very small area of extreme (D3) drought developed at the end of the month. Normal rainfall across this area for July is 4 to 5 inches. Actually, only 1 to 2 inches fell during the month. Crops were put under more stress from the dry and hot conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields when harvest arrives in early Fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was 1 to 3 inches below normal for the month with the county seeing extreme (D3) drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down near the intersection of Eureka and Hubbard Roads about 4 miles east-northeast of Pope in Panola County. The tornado snapped trees and bolted down playground equipment was thrown. A dumpster was turned over and power lines were also downed.

A slow moving weather pattern developed during the first two days of May and supported a couple rounds of thunderstorms both on Saturday and Sunday. While our area was spared the larger severe weather outbreak, we did manage to have a few rounds of severe storms on Saturday evening and then again Sunday afternoon. The most significant storms occurred Sunday afternoon and brought large hail, between golf ball and baseball sized, to portions of central Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 60 mph wind gust was reported in northern Webster County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall during the months of June and July brought severe (D2) drought conditions by the end of July to portions of Northwest Mississippi. The biggest impact from the drought was on agriculture as many crops suffered due to the lack of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall brought severe drought conditions to Coahoma County. Crops suffered from the lack of rainfall as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved southeast into the Mid-South and interacted with remnants of Tropical Depression Bonnie as the remnants moved north. Numerous showers and thunderstorms broke out during the afternoon and evening hours of July 25th-July 26th, 2010 as a result. Some storms became severe and produced damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees and power lines from Tupelo to Saltillo. A century-old Pin Oak fell on a house in Tupelo and caused extensive roof damage. The tree also took out the windshield and driver's side window of a pickup truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure intensified over the Mid-South at the end of July producing high heat indices between 105-109 degrees. These conditions continued into the early part of August. One death occurred as a result of the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings ranged between 105-109 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree snapped and fell on to an outbuilding off Highway 35. Another tree was blown down at the Highway 35 and I-20 interchange.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime sized hail was reported at exit 30 on Interstate 55 south near Bogue Chitto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-South was underneath northwest flow aloft during the period between July 11th, 2010 and July 13th, 2010. Several upper level disturbances quickly moved through the flow setting off complexes of showers and thunderstorms. Some of these showers and thunderstorms became severe and produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees, tree limbs. and power lines in Ripley. A roof was blown off of a mobile home on Tippah Lake Road. Winds were estimated between 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the Mid-South during the morning hours of July 16th, 2010. The front continued to push southeast through the morning hours of July 17th. Showers and thunderstorms develop along and ahead of the front. Some storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Booneville. Water flooded 2nd Street near the hospital and Industrial Park Road. Keenum Stadium on the Northeast Mississippi Community College campus was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very dry conditions continued across west-central portions of Mississippi during July. The extended dry stretch resulted in an area of severe (D2) drought developing across parts of the Mississippi Delta. Embedded in that area, a very small area of extreme (D3) drought developed at the end of the month. Normal rainfall across this area for July is 4 to 5 inches. Actually, only 1 to 2 inches fell during the month. Crops were put under more stress from the dry and hot conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields when harvest arrives in early Fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was 1 to 3 inches below normal for the month with the county seeing severe (D2) drought conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A four day stretch of extreme temperatures occurred across the region to start off the month of August. High pressure was firmly entrenched across the southeast and allowed temperatures to soar into the triple digits across much of the region. Across the NWS Jackson, MS forecast area, 19 record highs were set between August 1st and 4th. On August 2nd, the 2nd warmest average temperature was recorded. The low was 78 and the high 105, this resulted in an average temperature of 91.5 degrees. Additionally, relatively high humidity levels made conditions even more oppressive, with heat index readings surpassing 110 degrees in many areas. This extreme heat resulted in 3 fatalities across the forecast area.

An upper level ridge of high pressure further intensified over the Mid-South continuing into the middle of August. The upper ridge of high pressure produced above normal temperatures over the area. The combination of high humidity and above normal temperatures produced brutal heat indices of 105-120 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings reached between 110 and 120 degrees during the afternoon and early evening hours.

The low was 78 and the high 105, this resulted in an average temperature of 91.5 degrees. Additionally, relatively high humidity levels made conditions even more oppressive, with heat index readings surpassing 110 degrees in many areas. This extreme heat resulted in 3 fatalities across the forecast area.

The combination of high humidity and above normal temperatures produced brutal heat indices of 105-120 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings ranged from 105 to 109 degrees during the afternoon and early evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely warm and moist airmass was in place over the region on the morning of August 15th. Abundant sunshine during the morning hours led to the development of extremely high atmospheric instability by early afternoon. This environment led to numerous severe thunderstorms throughout the day, continuing into the evening hours. Numerous damaging wind reports were received, including several reports of damage caused by a strong outflow boundary that surged south across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd and 3rd. Some of the storms produced wind damage as this activity occurred in triple digit heat. Some lightning damage was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 18 wheeler was blown over along Interstate 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued during the month of August which expanded severe (D2) drought conditions to additional portions of Northwest Mississippi. The biggest impact from the drought was on agriculture as many crops suffered due to the lack of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of temperatures near 100 degrees contributed to two deaths from heat stroke in the Gulfport area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Harrison County Coroner stated that two deaths in a mobile home on Smith Road near Canal Road were caused by heat stroke. High temperatures at Gulfport Airport, approximately 3 miles away, were between 98 and 102 degrees from July 29 through August 2. Bodies were discovered on August 4, but deaths occurred several days prior to that. Date of deaths was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms occurred across the region on August 13th and 14th. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and torrential rain, which resulted in some flash flooding.

Numerous streets were under water with several roads closed. Traffic had to be redirected around town. A few businesses were flooded due to the high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved southeast into the Mid-South and interacted with remnants of Tropical Depression Bonnie as the remnants moved north. Numerous showers and thunderstorms broke out during the afternoon and evening hours of July 25th-July 26th, 2010 as a result. Some storms became severe and produced damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding near Verona. Highway 145 near the Research and Extension Center was flooded and impassable as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across west-central portions of Mississippi during September. The dry stretch resulted in severe (D2) drought conditions continuing across parts of the Mississippi Delta region despite a few locations receiving above normal rainfall for the month. Crops were put under more stress from the hot and dry conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields when harvest arrives in early Fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was 1 to 3 inches below normal for most of the county, with moderate drought conditions persisting for the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Tropical Depression 5 moved northward into the Mid-South during the day on August 18th, 2010. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the Tropical Depression 5. Some storms produced very heavy rain which caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced widespread flash flooding across Tippah County. County Road 200 and County Road 240 near Falkner were flooded as a result. In addition, County Road 237 was flooded along with several streets in Walnut. Several culverts were washed out in Tiplersville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Tropical Depression 5 moved northward into the Mid-South during the day on August 18th, 2010. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the Tropical Depression 5. Some storms produced very heavy rain which caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding along Highway 72 near the Union Center Fire Department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved southeast into the Mid-South and interacted with remnants of Tropical Depression Bonnie as the remnants moved north. Numerous showers and thunderstorms broke out during the afternoon and evening hours of July 25th-July 26th, 2010 as a result. Some storms became severe and produced damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as 4 inches of rain fell near Tremont resulting in flash flooding. Paaper Springs Road was flooded as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A four day stretch of extreme temperatures occurred across the region to start off the month of August. High pressure was firmly entrenched across the southeast and allowed temperatures to soar into the triple digits across much of the region. Across the NWS Jackson, MS forecast area, 19 record highs were set between August 1st and 4th. On August 2nd, the 2nd warmest average temperature was recorded. The low was 78 and the high 105, this resulted in an average temperature of 91.5 degrees. Additionally, relatively high humidity levels made conditions even more oppressive, with heat index readings surpassing 110 degrees in many areas. This extreme heat resulted in 3 fatalities across the forecast area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued through the month of September across much of North Mississippi. The lack of rainfall expanded severe (D2) drought conditions from just a small portion of Northwest Mississippi to more of Northwest Mississippi. The biggest impact from the drought was on agriculture as many crops suffered due to the lack of rainfall. Hydrological concerns started to become an issue by the end of the month as many lake and river levels dropped. Burn bans were issued for most of North Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions continued across Tunica County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Depression 5 (TD5) pushed northward over the western portion of the region during the period. On the 18th, TD5 caused some torrential downpours and extreme rainfall totals across a small portion of the forecast area which produced some substantial flooding over portions of southwest Mississippi. The rainfall totals were extreme across portions of Adams County, with a sizable portion of the county receiving 5 to 10 inches.||Early on the 19th, the remnants of TD5 caused some localized flooding in Scott County, which resulted in some evacuations and several flooded roads. During this event, rainfall totals ranged from 3 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As extreme rain occurred across the southern portion of the county, locations near and just to the south of Natchez received locally heavy rain between 2 and 6 inches. Several roads were flooded along with a few homes. A few of the roads had to be closed due to high water covering them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a weak front, strong upper disturbance, and daytime heating, increased instability and brought a round of severe storms to the region on the afternoon and evening of August 22. These storms produced wind damage along with isolated instances of flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A four day stretch of extreme temperatures occurred across the region to start off the month of August. High pressure was firmly entrenched across the southeast and allowed temperatures to soar into the triple digits across much of the region. Across the NWS Jackson, MS forecast area, 19 record highs were set between August 1st and 4th. On August 2nd, the 2nd warmest average temperature was recorded. The low was 78 and the high 105, this resulted in an average temperature of 91.5 degrees. Additionally, relatively high humidity levels made conditions even more oppressive, with heat index readings surpassing 110 degrees in many areas. This extreme heat resulted in 3 fatalities across the forecast area.

The remnants of Tropical Depression 5 (TD5) pushed northward over the western portion of the region during the period. On the 18th, TD5 caused some torrential downpours and extreme rainfall totals across a small portion of the forecast area which produced some substantial flooding over portions of southwest Mississippi. The rainfall totals were extreme across portions of Adams County, with a sizable portion of the county receiving 5 to 10 inches.||Early on the 19th, the remnants of TD5 caused some localized flooding in Scott County, which resulted in some evacuations and several flooded roads. During this event, rainfall totals ranged from 3 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall, between 3 and 6 inches, occurred across portions of Scott County during the morning hours of August 19th. This rainfall event resulted in several homes and businesses flooded along with 15 people being evacuated from high water. Several vehicles were trapped in flood waters and many roads and streets were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely warm and moist airmass was in place over the region on the morning of August 15th. Abundant sunshine during the morning hours led to the development of extremely high atmospheric instability by early afternoon. This environment led to numerous severe thunderstorms throughout the day, continuing into the evening hours. Numerous damaging wind reports were received, including several reports of damage caused by a strong outflow boundary that surged south across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large oak tree fell on a deck and damaged the deck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across west-central portions of Mississippi during September. The dry stretch resulted in severe (D2) drought conditions continuing across parts of the Mississippi Delta region despite a few locations receiving above normal rainfall for the month. Crops were put under more stress from the hot and dry conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields when harvest arrives in early Fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was below normal for most of the county, with severe drought conditions persisting for much of the county.

Abundant sunshine during the morning hours led to the development of extremely high atmospheric instability by early afternoon. This environment led to numerous severe thunderstorms throughout the day, continuing into the evening hours. Numerous damaging wind reports were received, including several reports of damage caused by a strong outflow boundary that surged south across the forecast area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A four day stretch of extreme temperatures occurred across the region to start off the month of August. High pressure was firmly entrenched across the southeast and allowed temperatures to soar into the triple digits across much of the region. Across the NWS Jackson, MS forecast area, 19 record highs were set between August 1st and 4th. On August 2nd, the 2nd warmest average temperature was recorded. The low was 78 and the high 105, this resulted in an average temperature of 91.5 degrees. Additionally, relatively high humidity levels made conditions even more oppressive, with heat index readings surpassing 110 degrees in many areas. This extreme heat resulted in 3 fatalities across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 48 year old man died while mowing his lawn. The coroner ruled that he died of a heart attack brought on by the excessive heat.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Copiah County icon was blown down from thunderstorm winds during the early evening of August 22nd. This icon was a barn which stood in a pasture just off Interstate 55 in central Copiah County. The Rainbow Barn as it was known, survived many storms over the years, even Hurricane Katrina. However, the weakened structure failed and was blown down by a storm on the evening of August 22nd.

An upper level ridge of high pressure further intensified over the Mid-South continuing from the end of July into the beginning of August. The upper ridge of high pressure produced above normal temperatures over the area. The combination of high humidity and above normal temperatures produced brutal heat indices of 105-120 degrees. Three deaths and one injury occurred as a result of the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings ranged between 110 and 115 degrees during the afternoon and early evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across central Mississippi during most of October. There were some rains that came late in the month which provided some temporary relief. Rainfall amounts ranged from a half to two inches with locally higher amounts. Most locations were 1 to 3 inches below normal for the month. The dry stretch resulted in severe (D2) drought conditions to expand during the month with even the portions of extreme (D3) drought conditions expanding as well. Crops were put under stress under the warm and dry conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields for harvest time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was from near normal to locally 2 inches below normal for the month. This resulted in moderate to severe drought conditions across the county by the end of the month. Some rain of 1 to 3 inches did affect the county toward the end of the month. This provided some relief to the drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent weather system, for late October, impacted our region and brought a round of showers and thunderstorms to the forecast area on October 24th and 25th. This system produced severe weather across a good portion of the South with nearly 300 severe weather reports stretching from Texas to northern Georgia. Across the NWS Jackson forecast area, a number of these storms reached severe limits for locations north of Interstate 20 and primarily produced damaging winds. A few additional instances of hail and flash flooding also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall caused some streets to flood across town, especially on Central Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure moved through the Mid-South during the evening hours on October 24th, 2010. Elevated thunderstorms developed in association with the upper trough. The thunderstorms continued into the early morning hours of October 25th, 2010. Some storms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds produced widespread damage along Lackey Road, McDuffie Cemetery Road, Markham Drive and Pickle Road, east of Aberdeen. Numerous trees were knocked down. Damage also occurred to a couple of sheds, a carport, and an outbuilding. A tree also fell down onto a house. Two other homes sustained minor damage as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms ahead of a cold front produced several reports of severe weather over the coastal counties of Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts blew down a tree and knocked out power to homes near Landon Road and Coleman Road in northern Gulfport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued through the month of October across much of North Mississippi. The lack of rainfall expanded severe (D2) drought conditions from Northwest Mississippi to portions of North Central Mississippi. The biggest impact from the drought was on agriculture as many crops suffered due to the lack of rainfall. Many pastures were in poor condition forcing farmers to feed livestock with baled hay. Hydrological concerns continued to be an issue as many lake and river levels dropped to low levels. Lack of moisture in the ground also caused several pipes to burst in the region. Burn bans were issued for most of North Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate drought conditions intensified to severe (D2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued through the month of October across much of North Mississippi. The lack of rainfall expanded severe (D2) drought conditions from Northwest Mississippi to portions of North Central Mississippi. The biggest impact from the drought was on agriculture as many crops suffered due to the lack of rainfall. Many pastures were in poor condition forcing farmers to feed livestock with baled hay. Hydrological concerns continued to be an issue as many lake and river levels dropped to low levels. Lack of moisture in the ground also caused several pipes to burst in the region. Burn bans were issued for most of North Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate drought conditions intensified to severe (D2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across central Mississippi during most of October. There were some rains that came late in the month which provided some temporary relief. Rainfall amounts ranged from a half to two inches with locally higher amounts. Most locations were 1 to 3 inches below normal for the month. The dry stretch resulted in severe (D2) drought conditions to expand during the month with even the portions of extreme (D3) drought conditions expanding as well. Crops were put under stress under the warm and dry conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields for harvest time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was 1 to locally 2 inches below normal for the month. This resulted in severe drought conditions across the county by the end of the month. Some rain of 1 to 2 inches did affect the county toward the end of the month. This provided some brief relief to the drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure further intensified over the Mid-South continuing from the end of July into the beginning of August. The upper ridge of high pressure produced above normal temperatures over the area. The combination of high humidity and above normal temperatures produced brutal heat indices of 105-120 degrees. Three deaths and one injury occurred as a result of the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings ranged between 110 and 115 degrees during the afternoon and early evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent weather system, for late October, impacted our region and brought a round of showers and thunderstorms to the forecast area on October 24th and 25th. This system produced severe weather across a good portion of the South with nearly 300 severe weather reports stretching from Texas to northern Georgia. Across the NWS Jackson forecast area, a number of these storms reached severe limits for locations north of Interstate 20 and primarily produced damaging winds. A few additional instances of hail and flash flooding also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of nickel to quarter sized hail fell across central Sunflower County with a track just to the south of Ruleville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms ahead of a cold front produced several reports of severe weather over the coastal counties of Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down near the intersection of Carr Bridge Road and Blackwell Farm Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure was exiting the area on the morning of October 20th. Sufficient low level moisture was in place overnight to allow a severe thunderstorm to track across south-central Mississippi during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages were reported across northwest Franklin County near Roxie due to downed power lines. A couple of trees were blown down near Garden City around the same time with one tree blocking a road just off Highway 33.

Very dry conditions continued across central Mississippi during most of October. There were some rains that came late in the month which provided some temporary relief. Rainfall amounts ranged from a half to two inches with locally higher amounts. Most locations were 1 to 3 inches below normal for the month. The dry stretch resulted in severe (D2) drought conditions to expand during the month with even the portions of extreme (D3) drought conditions expanding as well. Crops were put under stress under the warm and dry conditions. The likely impact will be less crop yields for harvest time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall ranged from a half inch above normal to locally 2 inches below normal for the month. This resulted in severe drought conditions across the county by the end of the month. Some rain of 1 to 3 inches with locally higher amounts did affect the county toward the end of the month. This provided some relief to the drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure further intensified over the Mid-South continuing from the end of July into the beginning of August. The upper ridge of high pressure produced above normal temperatures over the area. The combination of high humidity and above normal temperatures produced brutal heat indices of 105-120 degrees. Three deaths and one injury occurred as a result of the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings ranged between 110 and 115 degrees during the afternoon and early evening hours. One heat related injury occurred at the Greenbrook Park softball fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper ridge of high pressure built over the Mid-South from July 25th through July 26th, 2010. Meanwhile, a surface boundary sagged south into West Tennessee that allowed higher dewpoints to pool along the boundary. The combination of the heat from the ridge and the higher dewpoints produced heat index readings between 105-109 degrees across North Mississippi during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings ranged between 105-109 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tracked northeast into Tippah County from Benton County. The tornado continued northeast crossing into Hardeman County, Tennessee. Numerous homes were damaged along County Road 301 near the intersection of County Road 303, along Highway 72 east of Walnut and near Brownfield. In total 5 homes were destroyed. Trees and power lines were knocked down in the area as well.

Multiple tree limbs were down between Byram and Terry, with a few power outages reported around Byram. Large tree limbs were downed along Mason Blvd between Terry Road and south Frontage Road along Interstate 55.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid May storm system, and weak surface front, brought widespread showers and thunderstorms to the area. Many of these storms reached severe limits and produced large hail and damaging winds. A lone tornado also touched down in Jones County and caused minor damage.

A stationary front was located over the Mid-South June 2nd-June 3rd, 2010. The front was the focus of afternoon and evening thunderstorm development. Some of the storms became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
A frontal system brought showers and thunderstorms to the region on February 21st. Some of these thunderstorms brought some hail to portions of south and central Mississippi.

A potent mid May storm system, and weak surface front, brought widespread showers and thunderstorms to the area. Many of these storms reached severe limits and produced large hail and damaging winds. A lone tornado also touched down in Jones County and caused minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched northwest of Moselle, and was on the ground for 3.5 miles. The maximum path width was 75 yards, and the maximum sustained winds were 75 mph. A number of small trees and large limbs were blown down along the path. A windmill tower was also blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pushed through the Mid South during the day on May 20th, 2010. This front triggered showers and thunderstorms as it moved across Northern Mississippi during the afternoon hours. Some of these storms were severe with damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 64 mph wind gust was recorded at the Tupelo Airport. The hospital helicopter hangar was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper disturbance, combined with an unstable and moist air mass, and produced scattered severe storms across portions of central Mississippi on the 15th and 16th. These storms produced scattered reports of thunderstorm wind damage as well as some isolated occurrences of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few power lines were blown down which caused several power outages across Philadelphia and other parts of northwest Neshoba County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low combined with an unstable airmass and and gulf moisture to bring widespread thunderstorms to the region during the afternoon and evening of June 15th. Many of the storms reached severe limits and brought hail, damaging thunderstorm wind gusts, and isolated flash flooding over portions of central and south Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across Highway 37 near the Liberty Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the region on both July 25th and 26th. Each day, a few storms reached severe limits and produced some minor wind damage. Additionally, locally heavy rainfall occurred from a few of the slower moving storms which resulted in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few power lines were blown down across town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a weak front, strong upper disturbance, and daytime heating, increased instability and brought a round of severe storms to the region on the afternoon and evening of August 22. These storms produced wind damage along with isolated instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down around Bassfield on Rogers Road, Sims Lane, and Stubbs Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Depression 5 (TD5) pushed northward over the western portion of the region during the period. On the 18th, TD5 caused some torrential downpours and extreme rainfall totals across a small portion of the forecast area which produced some substantial flooding over portions of southwest Mississippi. The rainfall totals were extreme across portions of Adams County, with a sizable portion of the county receiving 5 to 10 inches.||Early on the 19th, the remnants of TD5 caused some localized flooding in Scott County, which resulted in some evacuations and several flooded roads. During this event, rainfall totals ranged from 3 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amount of 5.66 inches fell at the Bienville National Forest Raws site. It fell mainly between 5 am and 10 am cst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across Mississippi triggered thunderstorms that produced several reports of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down several trees in the Fort Adams area. Time of event estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm system moved across the region on April 24th and brought an outbreak of severe storms and tornadoes to the area. The most powerful and destructive storm during this event was the long track EF4 tornado which ripped a 149 mile path across the forecast area. In addition to this violent tornado, many other storms became severe and produced 5 additional tornadoes along with numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail. Of the 5 tornadoes, 3 of these were strong (EF2) and occurred across, Jasper, Newton, Lauderdale, Oktibbeha, and Clay Counties.

A warm and moist airmass over the region, coupled with a weak upper low to the east of the forecast area, triggered a few severe thunderstorms both afternoons. The storms primarily generated gusty winds although some hail up to 1 inch in diameter was found.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted and then fell through the roof of a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid May storm system, and weak surface front, brought widespread showers and thunderstorms to the area. Many of these storms reached severe limits and produced large hail and damaging winds. A lone tornado also touched down in Jones County and caused minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pushed north across the region during the afternoon and evening of April 30th. As this occurred, thunderstorms developed across portions of north-central Mississippi and produced locally heavy rainfall and some severe weather. Flash flooding occurred across portions of Carroll, Montgomery, and Webster Counties along with a few occurrences of hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded across the county with many roads washed out. District 2 was the hardest hit which covers most of the central section of the county. Four to 6 inches of rain was measured in this area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall caused drains to back up which resulted in several streets being under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Vigorous storm system combined with cold temperatures aloft and steep lapse rates to produce scattered severe storms during the early morning hours of March 12th. This event consisted of mostly hail producing storms across the eastern half of Mississippi.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down blocking Highway 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid May storm system, and weak surface front, brought widespread showers and thunderstorms to the area. Many of these storms reached severe limits and produced large hail and damaging winds. A lone tornado also touched down in Jones County and caused minor damage.

Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of August 5th. This round of storms essentially broke the heat wave which started the month. The atmosphere was primed for severe storms this day and several thunderstorms brought damaging winds, hail, and locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were down in Kosciusko. Power outages were reported across Attala County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours on June 26th, 2010 as a result of a very moist and unstable air mass combined with maximum daytime heating. The showers and thunderstorms would quickly dissipate after sunset with the loss of daytime heating. Some storms were severe producing damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding across parts of Western Alcorn County. Several roads were flooded including County Road 500, County Road 600 and Union Church Road. A couple of roads in the northwest corner of the county near the county line were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, moist and unstable airmass triggered a few thunderstorms producing torrential downpours over central Mississippi on June 29th and 30th. Flash flooding was the only severe weather reported during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing across Interstate 55 between mile markers 107 and 108. Numerous accidents were reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous afternoon thunderstorms developed across the region, with the majority occurring along and east of I-55. The storms produced scattered wind damage with downed trees and power lines being the primary type of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall caused several streets to flood in Cruger. Those streets were under a half a foot of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved southeast into the Mid-South and interacted with remnants of Tropical Depression Bonnie as the remnants moved north. Numerous showers and thunderstorms broke out during the afternoon and evening hours of July 25th-July 26th, 2010 as a result. Some storms became severe and produced damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees on Highway 25 just southwest of New Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely warm and moist airmass was in place over the region on the morning of August 15th. Abundant sunshine during the morning hours led to the development of extremely high atmospheric instability by early afternoon. This environment led to numerous severe thunderstorms throughout the day, continuing into the evening hours. Numerous damaging wind reports were received, including several reports of damage caused by a strong outflow boundary that surged south across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed along old Pickens Road and Highway 429.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent weather system, for late October, impacted our region and brought a round of showers and thunderstorms to the forecast area on October 24th and 25th. This system produced severe weather across a good portion of the South with nearly 300 severe weather reports stretching from Texas to northern Georgia. Across the NWS Jackson forecast area, a number of these storms reached severe limits for locations north of Interstate 20 and primarily produced damaging winds. A few additional instances of hail and flash flooding also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were down southwest of Caledonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent weather system, for late October, impacted our region and brought a round of showers and thunderstorms to the forecast area on October 24th and 25th. This system produced severe weather across a good portion of the South with nearly 300 severe weather reports stretching from Texas to northern Georgia. Across the NWS Jackson forecast area, a number of these storms reached severe limits for locations north of Interstate 20 and primarily produced damaging winds. A few additional instances of hail and flash flooding also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees, including some large trees, were reported down south of Ruleville. Six to eight utility poles were also snapped along Highway 49 West.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe storms developed over the ArkLaMiss region on January 20th and 21st. Reports of severe weather, including damaging winds, large hail, and flooding were widespread across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused water to flow across, and cover, one lane of Ridgewood Road near East County Line Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving weather pattern developed during the first two days of May and supported a couple rounds of thunderstorms both on Saturday and Sunday. While our area was spared the larger severe weather outbreak, we did manage to have a few rounds of severe storms on Saturday evening and then again Sunday afternoon. The most significant storms occurred Sunday afternoon and brought large hail, between golf ball and baseball sized, to portions of central Mississippi.

During the evening hours of November 29th and the overnight/early morning period of the 30th, the region was impacted by an outbreak of tornadoes. This outbreak brought 13 tornadoes to the forecast area. This occurred as potent storm system moved out of the Rockies and developed a strong area of low pressure across the Central Plains. This surface low caused a warm front to take shape and quickly lift northward across the forecast during the afternoon of the 29th. Warm and humid conditions pushed north, which led to unstable conditions over the region just ahead of the advancing strong cold front. The wind energy through the atmosphere was strong. The resulting wind shear (change in wind speed or direction with height) was strong and very favorable for rotating storms and supporting tornadoes. As the clash of systems occurred, a mix of supercell thunderstorms developed, along with a broken line of thunderstorms. A handful of these storms utilized the strong wind shear present and produced tornadoes. Overall, 13 tornadoes occurred with 5 being of the strong variety (EF2-EF3). The strongest tornado occurred over northwest Leake and southern Attala Counties. Here, an EF3 moved along a 10 mile path and destroyed numerous mobile homes, 6 people were injured. Four other strong tornadoes occurred and were all rated EF2. Two of these occurred in Yazoo County, one just southwest of Yazoo City and the other in Downtown Yazoo City. Numerous buildings were damaged along with many trees and power lines down. Another EF2 occurred in Starkville where it destroyed multiple mobile homes in town. Fifteen injuries occurred in this area. The final EF2 occurred in Smith County. This tornado had an 11 mile path which started just SW of Raleigh to 6 miles NE of Raleigh. One brick home sustained significant damage along with numerous other buildings and sheds damaged.||This outbreak broke a streak of 3 years when no tornadoes occurred during the month of November across Mississippi. Additionally, the last Fall outbreak of tornadoes occurred on November 24, 2004. Historically, November marks the peak of our 2nd severe weather season and ranks 3rd as the most active month for tornadoes in Mississippi, just a few behind March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Dowdle Road was under water near Wolf Road, outside Caledonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved into the Mid-South during the day on October 12th, 2010. Numerous thunderstorms developed in association with the upper low. Some storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell on Highway 61 near Dubbs in Tunica County.
